risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned to either the TM or health education (HE) arms using a stratified block design... Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician" Comment: The study explicitly reports random assignment using a stratified block design, with allocation performed by a biostatistician. This provides sufficient detail to indicate a proper random sequence generation method, so Low risk is appropriate.

Quote: "Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator." Comment: This explicitly states that the allocation schedule was concealed from those enrolling/assigning participants, which is sufficient evidence of allocation concealment and suggests recruiters could not foresee the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial." and "The control intervention was a cardiovascular health education program designed to match the format of the experimental intervention for instructional time, instructor attention, participant expectancy, social support, and other nonspecific factors." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the interventions were not blinded (single-blind only; investigators/data staff were blinded, but not participants/personnel). Because the study assessed subjective and behaviorally influenced outcomes in addition to clinical events (e.g. blood pressure, diet, exercise, anger, depression), knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcomes, so performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "Investigators, data collectors and data management staff were blinded to group assignment. Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial." and "All clinical end points were adjudicated by a blinded, independent reviewer who applied standardized and validated criteria." Comment: The outcome assessors for clinical events were an independent blinded reviewer, and the main outcomes were objective clinical endpoints (all-cause mortality, MI, stroke) determined from death certificates, National Death Index, hospital records, and blinded adjudication; these measurements are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "The rate of nonparticipation in the treatment groups was 19/99 or 19% in the TM group and 10/102 or 10% in the HE group, a nonsignificant difference (p=.07, Fishers’s exact test)." and "All primary and secondary outcomes were analyzed using the intention-to-treat principle." Comment: Although there was some attrition and 58 phase 1 subjects did not re-enroll in phase 2, the paper reports balanced/non-significant nonparticipation between groups, uses ITT, and for the main survival analysis includes follow-up from public records for deaths even among non-reenrolled subjects. The authors also state there was "no evidence of selective attrition," so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the result.
